flashcards.back: A heap-ordered binary tree where for every node, the s-value (shortest path to null) of the left child is >= the s-value of the right child. This ensures the rightmost path is always the shortest path in the tree.
For a node x, shortest(x) = length of the shortest path from x to any external (null) node. For null nodes, s-value = 0. For internal nodes, s(x) = 1 + min(s(left), s(right)).

To merge two min-leftist trees: (1) Compare roots, smaller root becomes new root. (2) Recursively merge the other tree with the right subtree of the winner. (3) If leftist property is violated, swap left and right children. (4) Update s-value.
Remove the root (which holds the minimum in a min-leftist tree) and merge its left and right subtrees. Time complexity: O(log n) since it reduces to a merge operation.
In a leftist tree with n nodes, the rightmost path has at most floor(log2(n+1)) nodes. This guarantees that merge, insert, and delete operations are all O(log n).
